initial_program:
% Grid dimensions
n_rows(N) :- N = #count{ R : input(R, _, _) }.
n_cols(N) :- N = #count{ C : input(_, C, _) }.
row(0..N-1) :- n_rows(N).
col(0..N-1) :- n_cols(N).

% Output cell definition (every combination of a row and a column is an output cell)
output_cell(R, C) :- row(R), col(C).

% Guess the two nonzero input cells.
% We assume the puzzle always has exactly two distinct nonzero colours.
% The guess forces an ordering R1 < R2 and C1 < C2 (so the two cells differ in both
% row and column  this covers the pattern used in the training examples 1 and 2).
2 { R1, C1, V1, R2, C2, V2 :
      input(R1, C1, V1), input(R2, C2, V2),
      R1 < R2,
      C1 < C2
    } 2 :- true.

% Column orientation: the two nonzero cells differ in column.
col_orient(R1,C1,V1,R2,C2,V2) :- C1 < C2, input(R1,C1,V1), input(R2,C2,V2).

% Helper predicates that bind the needed variables.
col_is_color1(C) :-
    col(C), col(C1), col(C2),
    ((C - C1) \ (C2 - C1)) \ (C2 - C1) = (C - C1) \ (C2 - C1).

col_is_color2(C) :-
    col(C), col(C1), col(C2),
    ((C - C1) \ (C2 - C1)) \ (C2 - C1) != (C - C1) \ (C2 - C1).

% Produce the output colour for each cell according to the column pattern.
output(R,C,V1) :- col_orient(R1,C1,V1,R2,C2,V2), col_is_color1(C).
output(R,C,V2) :- col_orient(R1,C1,V1,R2,C2,V2), col_is_color2(C).

% Each output cell must get exactly one colour.
1 { output(R, C, Colour) : colour(Colour) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

#show output/3.

rounds.program_after:
% Grid dimensions
n_rows(N) :- N = #count{ R : input(R, _, _) }.
n_cols(N) :- N = #count{ C : input(_, C, _) }.
row(0..N-1) :- n_rows(N).
col(0..N-1) :- n_cols(N).

% Output cell definition (every combination of a row and a column is an output cell)
output_cell(R, C) :- row(R), col(C).

% Guess the two nonzero input cells.
% We assume the puzzle always has exactly two distinct nonzero colours.
% The guess forces an ordering R1 < R2 and C1 < C2 (so the two cells differ in both
% row and column  this covers the pattern used in the training examples 1 and 2).
2 { guess(R1, C1, V1, R2, C2, V2) :
    input(R1, C1, V1), input(R2, C2, V2),
    R1 < R2,
    C1 < C2
} 2 :- true.

% Column orientation: the two nonzero cells differ in column.
col_orient(R1,C1,V1,R2,C2,V2) :- C1 < C2, input(R1,C1,V1), input(R2,C2,V2).

% Helper predicates that bind the needed variables.
col_is_color1(C) :-
    col(C), col(C1), col(C2),
    ((C - C1) \ (C2 - C1)) \ (C2 - C1) = (C - C1) \ (C2 - C1).

col_is_color2(C) :-
    col(C), col(C1), col(C2),
    ((C - C1) \ (C2 - C1)) \ (C2 - C1) != (C - C1) \ (C2 - C1).

% Produce the output colour for each cell according to the column pattern.
output(R,C,V1) :- col_orient(R1,C1,V1,R2,C2,V2), col_is_color1(C).
output(R,C,V2) :- col_orient(R1,C1,V1,R2,C2,V2), col_is_color2(C).

% Each output cell must get exactly one colour.
1 { output(R, C, Colour) : colour(Colour) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

#show output/3.

rounds.program_after:
% Grid dimensions
n_rows(N) :- N = #count{ R : input(R, _, _) }.
n_cols(N) :- N = #count{ C : input(_, C, _) }.
row(0..N-1) :- n_rows(N).
col(0..N-1) :- n_cols(N).

% Output cell definition (every combination of a row and a column is an output cell)
output_cell(R, C) :- row(R), col(C).

% Guess the two nonzero input cells.
% We assume the puzzle always has exactly two distinct nonzero colours.
% The guess forces an ordering R1 < R2 and C1 < C2 (so the two cells differ in both
% row and column  this covers the pattern used in the training examples 1 and 2).
2 { guess(R1, C1, V1, R2, C2, V2) :
    input(R1, C1, V1), input(R2, C2, V2),
    R1 < R2,
    C1 < C2
} 2 :- true.

% Column orientation: the two nonzero cells differ in column.
col_orient(R1,C1,V1,R2,C2,V2) :- C1 < C2, input(R1,C1,V1), input(R2,C2,V2).

% Helper predicates that bind the needed variables.
col_is_color1(C) :-
    col(C), col(C1), col(C2),
    ((C - C1) \ (C2 - C1)) \ (C2 - C1) = (C - C1) \ (C2 - C1).

col_is_color2(C) :-
    col(C), col(C1), col(C2),
    ((C - C1) \ (C2 - C1)) \ (C2 - C1) != (C - C1) \ (C2 - C1).

% Produce the output colour for each cell according to the column pattern.
output(R,C,V1) :- R = R1, C = C1, col_orient(R1,C1,V1,R2,C2,V2), col_is_color1(C).
output(R,C,V2) :- R = R2, C = C2, col_orient(R1,C1,V1,R2,C2,V2), col_is_color2(C).

% Each output cell must get exactly one colour.
1 { output(R, C, Colour) : colour(Colour) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

#show output/3.

final_program:
% Grid dimensions
n_rows(N) :- N = #count{ R : input(R, _, _) }.
n_cols(N) :- N = #count{ C : input(_, C, _) }.
row(0..N-1) :- n_rows(N).
col(0..N-1) :- n_cols(N).

% Output cell definition (every combination of a row and a column is an output cell)
output_cell(R, C) :- row(R), col(C).

% Guess the two nonzero input cells.
% We assume the puzzle always has exactly two distinct nonzero colours.
% The guess forces an ordering R1 < R2 and C1 < C2 (so the two cells differ in both
% row and column  this covers the pattern used in the training examples 1 and 2).
2 { guess(R1, C1, V1, R2, C2, V2) :
    input(R1, C1, V1), input(R2, C2, V2),
    R1 < R2,
    C1 < C2
} 2 :- true.

% Column orientation: the two nonzero cells differ in column.
col_orient(R1,C1,V1,R2,C2,V2) :- C1 < C2, input(R1,C1,V1), input(R2,C2,V2).

% Helper predicates that bind the needed variables.
col_is_color1(C) :-
    col(C), col(C1), col(C2),
    ((C - C1) \ (C2 - C1)) \ (C2 - C1) = (C - C1) \ (C2 - C1).

col_is_color2(C) :-
    col(C), col(C1), col(C2),
    ((C - C1) \ (C2 - C1)) \ (C2 - C1) != (C - C1) \ (C2 - C1).

% Produce the output colour for each cell according to the column pattern.
output(R,C,V1) :- R = R1, C = C1, col_orient(R1,C1,V1,R2,C2,V2), col_is_color1(C).
output(R,C,V2) :- R = R2, C = C2, col_orient(R1,C1,V1,R2,C2,V2), col_is_color2(C).

% Each output cell must get exactly one colour.
1 { output(R, C, Colour) : colour(Colour) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

#show output/3.
